race.name: Ring of Kerry Charity Cycle
race.tagline: 170 km around Ireland's Iveragh Peninsula. 4,000+ riders. 1,200 volunteers. EUR 22 million raised since 1982. The Atlantic coast is the scenery. Charity is the point.

vitals.start_time: Rolling start 6:00-8:30 AM, official start 7:30 AM
vitals.registration: Online via ringofkerrycycle.ie. Cost: EUR 100 + 4% admin fee (~$108 USD). Opens early in the year, spaces limited to ~6,000.
vitals.prize_purse: None. This is a non-competitive charity event. No timing, no results.
vitals.aid_stations: 3 official food stations: Cahersiveen (72 km), Kenmare (136 km), Killarney (finish). Multiple water stations. 11 fixed bike repair stations plus mobile mechanical units. Medical stations and mobile ambulances throughout.
vitals.cutoff_time: All support systems stand down at 8:00 PM
distance_options: Full Ring of Kerry: ~170 km (106 mi), ~1,760m climbing

climate.primary: Atlantic maritime Ireland — mild, wet, windy, changeable
climate.description: July in County Kerry delivers Ireland's best weather, which still means preparing for everything. Temperatures sit at 13-19C (55-66F) with an average of 99mm of rainfall across the month and roughly 7 hours of daily sunshine. The Atlantic coast sections are exposed to whatever blows in off the ocean — headwinds, crosswinds, sudden rain squalls, or blazing sunshine that makes the Skellig Islands glow. The interior sections through Kerry's hills are more sheltered but can trap humidity. A rain jacket is not optional. It is mandatory kit. The organisers know this. You should too.
challenges: Atlantic weather systems: rain can arrive without warning on exposed coastal stretches
Wind exposure on the southern coastal road between Waterville and Caherdaniel
Temperature range of 10-20C (50-68F) — hypothermia risk if wet and windblown for hours
Four seasons in one ride is standard Irish operating procedure — pack layers

terrain.primary: Paved Irish roads circumnavigating the Iveragh Peninsula
terrain.surface: Public roads through County Kerry. Mix of N-roads and R-roads. Surface quality varies from smooth national routes to rougher regional roads with patching. Roads are closed to motor traffic on event day (counterclockwise direction only). Some narrow stretches through villages.
features: Coomakista Pass — the coastal climb south of Waterville with sweeping Atlantic views to the Skellig Islands
Moll's Gap — the highest point on the route at 260m, the most sustained climb of the day
Atlantic coast road — exposed seaboard riding between Cahersiveen and Kenmare with ocean on one side and Kerry's mountains on the other
Killarney National Park — the final approach through ancient oakwoods and lough-side roads
Roads closed to motor traffic in the direction of travel — counterclockwise around the Ring

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: Ireland's largest one-day cycling event. 44th edition in 2026. EUR 22M+ raised for 200+ charities since 1982. The community and charity dimensions are extraordinary — community=5 is earned, not given. T2 by score (61).
gravel_god_rating.score_note: community=5 for the defining charity-cycling event in Ireland — 1,200 volunteers, 40+ charities, entire Kerry region mobilises. adventure=4 for the Iveragh Peninsula Atlantic coast scenery. experience=4 for the combination of mass-participation energy and Wild Atlantic Way landscape. field_depth=1 because this is explicitly non-competitive. cultural_impact=3 for four decades as a Kerry civic institution and national charity landmark.

logistics.explanation: Killarney is a well-established tourist town — hotels, B&Bs, hostels, restaurants, pubs. But getting there takes effort. Kerry Airport (KIR) has limited routes. Cork (ORK) is 90 minutes south. Shannon (SNN) is 2 hours north. Dublin (DUB) is 4 hours by car. Once you reach Killarney, the town is compact and walkable, and the event organisation is polished after 40+ years. But accommodation fills months ahead — this is peak Kerry tourist season — and the town's capacity is strained by thousands of cyclists plus normal July tourism. Book early or sleep in your car.

length.explanation: 170 km with 1,760m of climbing is a long day for a recreational charity event. Most riders take 6-10 hours. The distance alone separates it from the typical charity sportive that tops out at 100 km. This is not a casual spin — it demands genuine fitness and preparation. The 8 PM support cutoff adds implicit time pressure. For riders accustomed to European granfondos, it is moderate. For the average charity cyclist, it is a serious undertaking.

technicality.explanation: Paved roads throughout. No gravel, no off-road, no technical demands beyond basic bike handling on descents. The Irish regional roads are narrower than continental European routes, and surface quality varies, but you do not need any special skills. The only technical consideration is descending Moll's Gap and Coomakista on potentially wet roads — brake early and keep your weight back.

elevation.explanation: ~1,760m of climbing over 170 km is rolling with teeth. Coomakista and Moll's Gap are genuine efforts — Moll's Gap reaches 260m and the approach from Kenmare on poor road surface is grim. But these are 10-20 minute climbs, not Alpine passes. The cumulative elevation is honest but never approaches the severity of the Maratona, L'Etape, or even the Fred Whitton. This is hilly Ireland, not mountainous Europe.

climate.explanation: Atlantic Ireland in July is a coin flip. The best days deliver 18C sunshine with the Skellig Islands sharp on the horizon and the ocean a deep blue. The worst days deliver horizontal rain, 12C temperatures, and a headwind that turns the coastal road into a survival exercise. Both outcomes are equally probable. Average July rainfall in Kerry is 99mm and the long-term temperature average is 15C. The wind off the Atlantic is the variable that changes everything. You cannot prepare for certainty. You can only prepare for anything.

altitude.explanation: The highest point is Moll's Gap at 260m above sea level. Kerry's mountains look dramatic but they are low by international standards. Altitude is a non-factor. Your lungs will not notice.

adventure.explanation: The Iveragh Peninsula is stunning. The Wild Atlantic Way coastal road between Waterville and Caherdaniel delivers views of the Skellig Islands — the jagged rock pinnacles rising from the Atlantic that served as Luke Skywalker's exile. The Ring of Kerry is one of Ireland's great scenic drives for good reason. Mountains plunge to the sea. Stone walls line the road. Ancient forts dot the hillsides. The Killarney National Park approach through oakwoods and past the Lakes of Killarney is genuinely beautiful. It does not quite reach a 5 because the route is a well-trodden tourist circuit, not a wilderness discovery — but the scenery is world-class.

prestige.explanation: Ireland's biggest one-day cycling event. Running since 1982. The Taoiseach has ridden it. EUR 22 million raised for charity. It is a national institution in Ireland and well-known across UK and European charity cycling circles. Internationally? Most cyclists outside the British Isles have never heard of it. It does not carry the cachet of L'Etape du Tour or the Cape Town Cycle Tour. But within Ireland and the charity cycling world, this is the flagship.

race_quality.explanation: This is not a race. There is no timing, no results, no competition. It is a supported charity cycle — and on those terms, it is excellently organised. 1,200+ volunteers. Three staffed food stations. Eleven fixed bike repair stations plus mobile units. Medical teams with ambulances. Roads closed to motor traffic in the direction of travel. After 44 editions, the machine runs smoothly. But the absence of any competitive element — no chip timing, no age groups, no results board — keeps this at 3. It is a superb charity ride, not a race.

experience.explanation: The combination of Atlantic coast scenery, mass-participation energy, and collective charity purpose creates something genuinely special. You are riding 170 km around one of Europe's most beautiful peninsulas with thousands of people who are there for the same reason — to raise money and ride together. The food station at Cahersiveen buzzes with camaraderie. The climb over Coomakista rewards with Skellig views. The finish back into Killarney, with its National Park and Victorian charm, is a proper celebration. On a good weather day, this is one of the great European cycling experiences. On a bad weather day, the shared suffering creates its own bond.

community.explanation: This is where the Ring of Kerry Charity Cycle is genuinely world-class. The entire event exists for community. 1,200 volunteers from Kerry organisations. 40+ charities supported annually. EUR 22 million raised over four decades for hospitals, special needs facilities, suicide prevention, mountain rescue, cancer support, and end-of-life care. The towns along the route come out to cheer. Local businesses support riders. The post-ride atmosphere in Killarney is a celebration of collective effort, not individual achievement. There is no cycling event in Ireland — and very few anywhere — where the community dimension is this deep, this sustained, and this genuine.

field_depth.explanation: There is no field depth because there is no competition. No timing. No seeding. No elite wave. No results. Riders range from seasoned club cyclists to first-timers on borrowed bikes. The organisers explicitly state this is a non-competitive event. Everyone rides at their own pace for their chosen charity. This is by design, not by deficiency — but a 1 is the honest score for competitive depth.

value.explanation: EUR 100 for 170 km of supported riding with three food stations, eleven mechanical support points, medical coverage, road closures, and 1,200 volunteers is strong value. And the entry fee goes to charity — it is not profit for an event company. You are paying to ride AND funding the charitable mission. The only reason this is not a 5 is that the fee is not trivial for a non-competitive event, and the food stations can run low for late finishers. But the value proposition — scenic route, comprehensive support, charitable purpose — is excellent.

expenses.explanation: Ireland is not cheap. Killarney hotels during Ring of Kerry weekend run EUR 150-300/night and book out months ahead. B&Bs are EUR 80-150. Flights to Kerry are limited and expensive; Cork or Shannon require car hire. Dublin is a 4-hour drive. Budget EUR 500-1,000 for an Irish-based rider (accommodation, fuel, food, entry), EUR 1,500-3,000 for an international trip. The favourable event date in peak tourist season means competing with non-cycling tourists for every bed and restaurant table in Kerry.

biased_opinion.summary: The Ring of Kerry Charity Cycle is not a race. It has no timing chips, no results board, no podium. What it has is 44 years of history, EUR 22 million raised for 200+ charities, 1,200 volunteers who make the roads of Kerry safe for thousands of cyclists, and 170 km of Atlantic coast scenery that ranks among the most beautiful cycling routes in Europe. The Iveragh Peninsula delivers the Skellig Islands, Coomakista's ocean views, Moll's Gap, and the approach through Killarney National Park. The climbing is honest but not savage. The weather is a gamble with the Atlantic. The community spirit is not manufactured — it is the DNA of the event. Founded in 1982, this is Ireland's largest one-day cycling event and one of Europe's most significant charity rides. You do not ride the Ring of Kerry to prove anything. You ride it to contribute to something larger than yourself, and the Atlantic scenery is the reward for showing up.
strengths: Community and charity purpose: EUR 22M+ raised over 44 years — this is one of the most impactful charity cycling events in Europe
Atlantic coast scenery: the Iveragh Peninsula, Skellig Islands, and Killarney National Park provide genuine visual drama
Comprehensive support: 1,200 volunteers, 3 food stations, 11 bike repair stations, full medical coverage
Mass-participation atmosphere: thousands of riders sharing the road for a common charitable cause
weaknesses: No competitive element: no timing, no results — if you want racing, look elsewhere
Atlantic weather: rain and wind on the coastal sections can turn the day from scenic to grim
Accommodation pressure: Killarney in peak July is expensive and books out early
Remote location: Kerry requires effort to reach — no major international airport nearby
biased_opinion.bottom_line: Ireland's greatest day on a bike. Not because of the climbing, the speed, or the competition — but because 170 km of Atlantic coast scenery shared with thousands of riders for charity creates something that pure racing never will.

final_verdict.one_liner: 170 km around the Iveragh Peninsula. Ireland's biggest one-day cycling event. EUR 22 million raised. The Atlantic coast is the scenery. Charity is the point.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want one of Europe's great scenic charity rides — the Iveragh Peninsula coastline is spectacular. Yes if community and charitable purpose matter as much as personal achievement. Skip if you need competition, timing, or race results. Skip if Atlantic weather uncertainty is a dealbreaker for a trip to western Ireland.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a UK/Ireland sportive with more competitive edge: try the Etape Caledonia. If you want a massive charity cycling event with climbing: try Alpe d'HuZes. If you want a world-scale mass-participation ride with Atlantic scenery: try the Cape Town Cycle Tour.

logistics.airport: Kerry Airport (KIR) — limited routes, 20 minutes from Killarney. Cork Airport (ORK) — 90 minutes south, more international options. Shannon Airport (SNN) — 2 hours north. Dublin Airport (DUB) — 4 hours by car/bus via motorway. Car hire recommended from any airport except Kerry.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Killarney is the base. Hotels, B&Bs, guesthouses, and hostels are plentiful but fill fast for Ring of Kerry weekend — book 3-6 months ahead. Kenmare and Killorglin are nearby alternatives. Some riders stay in Tralee (30 minutes) for cheaper options. Camping available at local sites. Expect EUR 150-300/night for hotels, EUR 80-150 for B&Bs during event weekend.
logistics.food: Killarney has excellent pubs and restaurants — Irish seafood, lamb, and traditional fare. Kerry Lamb and Dingle Bay crab are local specialities. Pre-ride carb loading is well-served by the town's Italian restaurants and gastropubs. On course: 3 official food stations provide food and water. Organisers recommend bringing your own supplies as stocks deplete throughout the day.
logistics.packet_pickup: Registration and wristband collection in Killarney in the days before the event. The wristband grants access to food stations. Check the official site for specific dates and locations.
logistics.parking: Event parking available in Killarney. The start is in Killarney town centre. Arrive early — roads close for the event. Many riders walk or cycle from accommodation to the start.

history.origin_story: In 1982, a group from Killarney had a straightforward idea: cycle the 170 km Ring of Kerry to raise money for charity. No timing chips. No performance data. No sponsorship deals. Just cyclists, a spectacular route, and a purpose larger than personal achievement. From that first ride, the event grew into Ireland's largest one-day cycling event and one of Europe's most significant charity cycling institutions. Over four decades, it has raised more than EUR 22 million for over 200 charities and voluntary organisations — funding hospital extensions, special needs facilities, suicide prevention services, mountain rescue, cancer support, and end-of-life care across Ireland.
notable_moments: 1982: First Ring of Kerry Charity Cycle — a group of Killarney cyclists ride 170 km to raise money for charity
Event grows from a local ride to Ireland's largest one-day cycling event over four decades
Peak participation of approximately 10,000 riders in the mid-2010s
2020-2021: COVID-19 pandemic disrupts the event — virtual editions and reduced numbers
2024: EUR 1,165,625 raised for 35 charities and community groups in a single edition
2025: Over EUR 1.19 million raised for 40+ charities with approximately 4,000 riders
Cumulative fundraising exceeds EUR 22 million for 200+ charities since inception
history.reputation: Ireland's cycling institution. What the London Marathon is to charity running in the UK, the Ring of Kerry Charity Cycle is to charity cycling in Ireland — a national event that transcends sport. The Taoiseach has ridden it. The entire county mobilises for it. The money it raises changes lives across Ireland.

course_description.character: A counterclockwise loop around the Iveragh Peninsula on Ireland's Atlantic seaboard. The route leaves Killarney heading west through Killorglin, follows the coast south through Cahersiveen, Waterville, and Caherdaniel, climbs Coomakista for Skellig views, continues east through Sneem to Kenmare, then returns to Killarney via Moll's Gap — the highest and toughest climb of the day. The terrain is rolling throughout with two significant climbs. The character alternates between exposed Atlantic coast road and sheltered inland valleys. It is not an extreme route by European granfondo standards, but 170 km with 1,760m of climbing and the possibility of Atlantic headwinds makes it a demanding day for recreational riders.

suffering_zones.label: Killarney to Killorglin
suffering_zones.desc: The mass start from Killarney town. Thousands of riders filter through the streets and head northwest toward Killorglin. The road is flat to gently rolling through green Kerry farmland. Legs are fresh, the group is dense, and the pace is social. The real riding begins after Killorglin when the route turns south toward the coast.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Flat to gently rolling, N-road, wide surface

suffering_zones.label: Killorglin to Cahersiveen
suffering_zones.desc: The route follows the northern coast of the Iveragh Peninsula westward. The Atlantic appears. The road undulates through small Kerry villages — Glenbeigh, Kells, Cahersiveen. The first food station at Cahersiveen (72 km) is a welcome refuelling stop. Wind can be a factor on the exposed coastal stretches. The pace settles into the day's rhythm.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Rolling coastal road, exposed to Atlantic wind

suffering_zones.label: Waterville to Coomakista
suffering_zones.desc: South of Waterville, the route climbs to Coomakista Pass. This is the scenic climax of the ride. From the summit, the Skellig Islands — the jagged rock pinnacles rising from the Atlantic where early Christian monks lived in stone beehive huts — are visible on clear days. The climb is moderate but the views demand you stop, look, and absorb what 1,400 years of Atlantic weather does to rock. The descent toward Caherdaniel and Derrynane is fast and coastal.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Moderate climb, ~180m elevation, exposed to wind

suffering_zones.label: Sneem to Kenmare
suffering_zones.desc: The colourful village of Sneem marks the transition from coast to interior. The road follows the Kenmare River estuary eastward. The terrain rolls through pastoral Kerry countryside. Fatigue is building — 120+ km in the legs and the biggest climb still ahead. The Kenmare food station (136 km) provides the last proper refuelling before Moll's Gap.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Rolling estuary road, sheltered from Atlantic wind

suffering_zones.label: Moll's Gap
suffering_zones.desc: The final significant climb and the highest point on the route at 260m. The approach from Kenmare is sustained and the road surface deteriorates — rough tarmac and patching for 4 km of steady gradient. Moll's Gap is where the day breaks riders who started too fast or ate too little. It is not Alpine steep, but at 140 km into the ride, it does not need to be. The summit delivers views across the Killarney Valley and the descent into Killarney National Park is a proper reward.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Sustained climb, moderate gradient, rough surface, ~260m summit

suffering_zones.label: Killarney National Park to Finish
suffering_zones.desc: The descent from Moll's Gap drops into Killarney National Park — ancient oakwoods, the Lakes of Killarney, and Ladies View lookout. The road is sheltered and the scenery shifts from wild Atlantic to gentle woodland. The final kilometres back into Killarney are flat and the finish-line atmosphere in town is a celebration. You are done. The Ring is closed.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Descent then flat, sheltered parkland roads

course_description.signature_challenge: Moll's Gap at 140 km — the highest point on the route with poor road surface and steady gradient. It is the moment when under-prepared riders discover that 170 km of Atlantic coast riding has consequences. The climb itself is moderate, but the context is not.

guide_variables.race_terrain: Paved Irish roads circumnavigating the Iveragh Peninsula — rolling coastal and inland roads with two significant climbs
guide_variables.race_weather: Atlantic maritime: 10-20C (50-68F), variable — rain, wind, and sunshine are all probable in any combination
race_challenges: 170 km distance: a long day for recreational riders, especially in changeable weather
Atlantic wind on exposed coastal sections between Cahersiveen and Kenmare
Moll's Gap at 140 km: sustained climb on poor road surface with 140 km of fatigue in the legs
Weather variability: rain gear and layering strategy are mandatory, not optional

non_negotiables.why: Kerry in July averages 99mm of rainfall. A quality rain jacket is mandatory event kit. Full-finger gloves, arm warmers, and a vest should be in your jersey pockets or support vehicle. Train in the rain if possible — wet descents off Moll's Gap and Coomakista on narrow roads require confidence. The organisers will not let you start without appropriate gear.

non_negotiables.requirement: Endurance base — minimum one 150+ km training ride on rolling terrain
non_negotiables.why: 170 km is a long day for most recreational cyclists. The terrain never truly flattens out — you are either climbing, descending, or rolling for the entire route. The cumulative fatigue at 140 km when Moll's Gap arrives is the test. If your longest ride is 100 km, Moll's Gap will be unpleasant.

non_negotiables.requirement: Nutrition strategy tested on long training rides
non_negotiables.why: Three food stations across 170 km means long gaps between official fuelling points. The organisers recommend carrying your own supplies. Plan to eat 60-90g of carbohydrate per hour and carry food between stations. Late finishers report food stations running low — do not rely solely on event nutrition.
